title: Interior Painting

budget.description: Walls only, 1 coat over existing paint, builder-grade paint, light prep for a 2,000 sq ft home

midRange.description: Walls and ceilings, 2 coats, quality paint (Behr or PPG), light prep and patching for a 2,000 sq ft home

premium.description: All surfaces including trim, doors, and ceilings, full prep and priming, premium paint (Benjamin Moore or Sherwin-Williams), 2 coats, color consultation for a 2,000 sq ft home

costFactors.title: Prep Work
costFactors.description: Prep eats 30-50% of a painter's labor time. Walls in good shape need just a quick wash and light sanding. Walls with cracks, nail holes, peeling paint, or water stains need patching, skim coating, sanding, and priming - all before a single coat of color goes on. A home with wallpaper removal can add $1-$3 per square foot on top of painting costs.

costFactors.title: Ceiling Height and Layout
costFactors.description: Standard 8-foot ceilings are the baseline. Vaulted or cathedral ceilings (10-20 feet) add 25-50% to labor costs because of scaffolding setup and slower work at height. Stairwells with tall walls are especially expensive. Lots of windows, doors, and built-ins also slow painters down due to cutting-in and taping.

costFactors.title: Number of Colors
costFactors.description: Every color change means stopping, cleaning tools, masking edges, and starting again. A whole-house job with one color throughout is significantly faster than painting each room a different color. Accent walls add $50-$150 per wall in labor. If you want 8 different colors across your home, expect to pay 15-25% more than a single-color job.

costFactors.title: Paint Quality
costFactors.description: Builder-grade paint runs $15-$25 per gallon and requires more coats for solid coverage. Mid-range paints like Behr Marquee or PPG Diamond cost $30-$50 per gallon and cover in fewer coats. Premium paints from Benjamin Moore (Regal, Aura) or Sherwin-Williams (Emerald, Duration) run $50-$80+ per gallon but offer superior coverage, washability, and durability. A typical 2,000 sq ft home needs 15-25 gallons.

costFactors.title: Surfaces Included
costFactors.description: Walls-only is the cheapest option at $2-$6 per square foot of floor space. Adding ceilings increases cost by 30-40%. Trim, baseboards, crown molding, and door/window casings add another $1-$3 per linear foot. Painting interior doors adds $75-$150 per door. A full-scope job (walls, ceilings, all trim, doors) can cost 2-3 times a walls-only quote.

materialOptions.pros: Cheapest option, fine for rentals or quick refreshes
materialOptions.cons: Poor coverage (needs more coats), fades faster, harder to clean
materialOptions.bestFor: Rental properties, temporary refreshes, closets and utility rooms

materialOptions.pros: Good coverage in 1-2 coats, washable, wide color selection, paint-and-primer combos available
materialOptions.cons: Not as durable as premium, some colors need extra coats
materialOptions.bestFor: Most whole-house painting projects, best value for homeowners

materialOptions.pros: Excellent coverage, extremely durable and washable, rich color depth, best color accuracy
materialOptions.bestFor: High-traffic areas, kitchens, bathrooms, homes where you want the finish to last 10+ years

materialOptions.pros: No harmful fumes, safe for occupied homes, eco-friendly
materialOptions.cons: Some formulations sacrifice durability, slightly higher cost
materialOptions.bestFor: Nurseries, bedrooms, homes with children or pets, painting while living in the home

materialOptions.pros: Hard durable finish, self-leveling, washable, resists chipping
materialOptions.cons: Longer dry time, more technique-sensitive, higher cost
materialOptions.bestFor: Trim, baseboards, doors, cabinets - any high-touch surface

northeast.label: Northeast
northeast.note: Higher labor rates in metro areas like NYC and Boston

westCoast.label: West Coast
westCoast.note: Highest painter wages nationally, strong VOC regulations

southeast.label: Southeast
southeast.note: Lower labor costs, large contractor pool

mountainWest.label: Mountain West
mountainWest.note: Rising costs in growing metro areas

diyTasks: Moving furniture and laying drop cloths
Painting closets, utility rooms, and low-visibility areas
proOnlyTasks: High cathedral ceilings and stairwells (safety risk)
Extensive drywall repair and skim coating
Lead paint abatement in pre-1978 homes
Spray finishing for large areas or cabinets
diyVsPro.diyRisks: The main risk is time and quality, not safety. A professional crew paints a 2,000 sq ft house in 3-5 days - most homeowners take 2-4 weekends. Poor taping leads to messy edges. Skipping primer over patches leaves visible spots. The DIY savings are real ($3,000-$6,000 on a typical house), but only if you're willing to invest the time and do proper prep.

savingsTips: Do your own prep work. Taping, patching nail holes, moving furniture, and laying drop cloths account for 30-50% of a painter's labor time. Handle it yourself and negotiate a lower rate.
Paint rooms yourself that don't need perfection. Closets, utility rooms, basements, and garages are great DIY candidates. Save the pro for living areas and high ceilings.
Limit the number of colors. Every color change adds setup time. Choosing one neutral for all walls and one accent color keeps labor costs down by 15-25%.
Buy paint during seasonal sales. Sherwin-Williams runs 30-40% off sales several times a year. Benjamin Moore dealers offer annual promotions. Big-box stores discount around holidays.
Use paint-and-primer combos to skip a separate priming coat. Products like Behr Marquee or Benjamin Moore Regal cover in one coat on previously painted walls, cutting labor time.
Get quotes in the off-season. Painters are slowest in late fall and winter (November-February). You'll get better rates and faster scheduling.
Ask about flat or matte finishes for ceilings and low-traffic rooms. They're cheaper to apply because imperfections are less visible, reducing prep time.
Supply the paint yourself. Some painters mark up paint 20-30%. Buy it yourself during a sale and save $200-$500 on a whole-house job.

contractorQuestions.question: What's included in your quote - walls only, or walls, ceilings, and trim?
contractorQuestions.why: The scope difference between walls-only and all-surfaces can double the price. Make sure you're comparing apples to apples between quotes.

contractorQuestions.question: How many coats are included, and what prep work is in the price?
contractorQuestions.why: Some quotes assume one coat with no prep. A proper job includes patching, sanding, priming problem areas, and two coats of finish paint.

contractorQuestions.question: What paint brand and product line will you use?
contractorQuestions.why: There's a huge quality difference between builder-grade and premium paint. If a quote seems low, they may be using cheap paint that won't hold up.

contractorQuestions.question: How do you protect floors, furniture, and fixtures?
contractorQuestions.why: Professional painters should use drop cloths, plastic sheeting, and proper masking. Paint drips on hardwood floors or countertops are expensive mistakes.

contractorQuestions.question: Do you include moving furniture back, or is that my responsibility?
contractorQuestions.why: Some crews move furniture to the center and cover it. Others expect empty rooms. Clarify this upfront to avoid day-of surprises.

contractorQuestions.question: How do you handle touch-ups after the job?
contractorQuestions.why: Good painters do a walkthrough and fix any missed spots, drips, or uneven coverage before final payment. Get this in writing.

contractorQuestions.question: Are your workers employees or subcontractors, and are they insured?
contractorQuestions.why: If an uninsured subcontractor falls off a ladder in your home, you could be liable. Verify insurance coverage before work begins.

secondaryKeywords: how much does it cost to paint a house interior
interior painting price per square foot
cost to paint inside of house
seo.metaDescription: How much does interior painting cost in 2026? National average is $7,500 for a 2,000 sq ft home. Get detailed cost breakdowns by scope, paint quality, and tier with our interactive calculator.
